evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's birth cohort has collapsed from 93,000 births in 2021 to approximately 72,000-73,000 in 2025, a decline of 22% over four years, directly shrinking the child population available to sustain village-level school cohorts.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) – Birth Statistics 2021–2025

evidence_appendix.claim: Total fertility rate in Hungary has fallen to 1.31 children per woman in 2025, down from 1.39 in 2024 and far below the 2.1 replacement level, with regional variation: Zala County experienced a 25% birth decline in H1 2024 compared to H1 2021, versus 22% in Budapest.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) – Fertility Rate and Regional Birth Data

evidence_appendix.claim: One-third of Hungary's settlements have populations below 500 inhabitants; 645 localities have populations below 300, and 114 of those have under-18 populations below 10 percent. Economic assessment judges these villages unlikely to survive without significant development programs.

sources.title: KSH Regional Atlas; GKI Economic Research Assessment

evidence_appendix.claim: Age-structure divergence is acute and geographically clustered: 91 municipalities have average age under 35, while 42 municipalities have average age above 50 (primarily in Vas and Zala counties). The share of Hungarians under age 18 fell from 18% (2010) to 16% (2020).

sources.title: Hungarian Regional Demographics – 2010–2020 Census Analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: In the smallest settlements, school viability does not decline gradually but can reach a zero-enrollment cliff. Garab (44 residents) closed its primary school because no children remained; the last birth in that village occurred nine years prior to reporting.

sources.title: Field reporting – Village of Garab case study

evidence_appendix.claim: Rural school closures are causally linked to accelerated local population decline in a Danish quasi-experimental study: a 7.6 percentage point population decline over 10 years post-closure, with effects emerging as delayed impact (statistically significant from year 6 onward) via social capital and housing-market lock-in mechanisms.

sources.title: Danish difference-in-differences study of rural school closures (2010–2011 cohort)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary exhibits negative demographic momentum with youth cohorts contracted to 14.4% of the population, presaging structural fiscal and staffing pressures that constrain school-network decisions across regions independent of whether individual schools remain open.

sources.title: Hungarian Demographic Structure and Fiscal Impact Analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: International evidence on whether school consolidation produces per-pupil cost savings is contested: rural New York studies show 31.5-61.7% net savings after adjustment costs, but multi-state U.S. research found no statistically significant relationship between enrollment and per-pupil cost, and a Texas study of 20 consolidated districts found no post-consolidation savings.

sources.title: Duncombe & Yinger (2003, 2005); Jewell (1989); Texas consolidation analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: School consolidation produces transportation cost offsets that reduce net fiscal savings: small/sparse districts systematically spend more on student transportation and federal transportation funds per pupil, creating an opportunity cost rarely fully counted in closure proposals.

sources.title: U.S. rural education economics literature on transportation burden

evidence_appendix.claim: Evidence on whether school closure causes further population decline is genuinely mixed: studies in Saxony and Sweden find no significant connection between closure and depopulation, while a Finnish difference-in-differences study (2010-2018) found closures were associated with greater subsequent population decrease than pre-closure trends.

sources.title: Barakat (2014, 2015); Amcoff (2012); Finnish closure study (2010-2018)

evidence_appendix.claim: School closure/retention decisions carry distributional consequences for ethnic and socioeconomic groups: village segregation via 'white flight' of non-Roma pupils to other schools is documented, Roma children constitute 45% of students in segregated schools or classrooms nationally, and consolidation/closure removes local options most accessible to poorer and Roma families with lower mobility.

sources.title: Humanium ECtHR documentation; Hungarian education segregation studies

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's per-pupil spending is comparatively low internationally at approximately $7,115 USD equivalent (rank 25 of 29 OECD/partner countries, 2022), constraining fiscal slack available to subsidize higher per-pupil overhead costs of small rural schools.

sources.title: OECD education spending comparison (2022)

evidence_appendix.claim: Governance system instability itself generates resource costs distinct from demographic drivers: the system oscillated between decentralization (pre-2013), centralization (2013), and partial re-decentralization (2017+), with each transition creating adjustment costs in staff, administration, and capital planning regardless of closure/retention decisions.

sources.title: Hungarian school governance timeline (municipal → KLIK → district centres reform sequence)

evidence_appendix.claim: Primary school enrollment in Hungary is declining nationally by approximately 4% per annum, with accelerated decline in rural areas due to youth migration to cities for employment

sources.title: Hungarian education statistics and rural migration trends

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary operates a centralized primary and secondary school system since 2013, with the Klebelsberg School Maintainer Centre and 58 deconcentrated school district directorates controlling all public school closures and staffing, with school directors appointed by the education minister

sources.title: Hungarian school centralization and governance structure post-2013

evidence_appendix.claim: Some segregated village schools in Hungary have been transferred to church maintainers rather than closed, with churches taking over schools and government potentially avoiding responsibility for segregation problems

sources.title: Church takeover of segregated schools in Hungary - campaigner reporting

evidence_appendix.claim: Church schools in Hungary serve a more socioeconomically advantaged student intake on average compared to public schools, with family-background status index of 0.18 in church schools versus minus 0.05 in public schools at grade 6 in 2017

sources.title: Hungarian church and public school composition analysis 2017

evidence_appendix.claim: Keeping multiple small village schools open historically limited within-school segregation but did not prevent between-settlement segregation, while territorial concentration of Roma population is increasing ghetto schools primarily in villages, with about half located in North-East Hungary

sources.title: Kertesi and colleagues segregation analysis; Hungarian education equity research

evidence_appendix.claim: Villages with primary schools do not show clearly better socioeconomic conditions overall—they demonstrate better demographic performance and partly better living/infrastructural conditions, but worse employment and welfare characteristics compared to villages without schools

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office village-level analysis; Hungary-Romania comparative school study

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary exhibits the largest urban-rural gap in education outcomes of all OECD countries before accounting for socioeconomic status, with some regions showing over 70% disadvantaged children and rural settlements in Western Transdanubia, Southern Baranya, and Northern Hungary recording institutional decline

sources.title: EU Monitor 2019; OECD education outcomes; Hungarian regional decline data 2020s

evidence_appendix.claim: Early school leaving rates are sharply higher in least-developed regions, villages, and among Roma populations (58.7% for Roma versus 9.3% for non-Roma in 2023), establishing unequal baseline future options before any closure policy

sources.title: EU Monitor education equity data 2023

evidence_appendix.claim: Teacher shortages are greatest in rural areas, with proportion of unqualified teachers in disadvantaged schools 2-3 times higher than in non-disadvantaged schools, and nationally half of all teachers are 50 or older while only 7.2% are under 30 in 2024/2025

sources.title: Hungarian teacher workforce composition and qualification analysis 2024

evidence_appendix.claim: Rural school bus commutes in general rural education literature exceed 30 minutes on average and are longer than urban counterparts, with longer commutes associated with skipped breakfast, lower grades, poorer fitness, fewer social activities, and worse study habits, disproportionately burdening lower-income households

sources.title: Journal of Rural Studies and general rural education research on commute burden

evidence_appendix.claim: The Finnish comprehensive school reform of 1972–1977 was deliberately piloted first in rural areas as proof-of-concept for equality before scaling to urban regions, treating rural viability as a precondition of system legitimacy rather than a residual problem.

sources.title: Finnish comprehensive school reform implementation sequence (1972–1977)

evidence_appendix.claim: Finnish state policy maintained exceptionally low pupil minimums for rural schools during the 1980s economic growth period, a reversible political choice not an automatic feature of the comprehensive model, enabling temporary protection of small schools.

sources.title: Finnish rural school network maintenance via pupil-minimum adjustment (1980s)

evidence_appendix.claim: During 1995–2003, the number of small rural Finnish primary schools with fewer than 50 pupils decreased dramatically, coinciding with municipal decentralization and the 1990s recession.

sources.title: Finnish rural school closure trends post-decentralization (1995–2003)

evidence_appendix.claim: Municipalities reported cost-saving as the primary rationale for rural school closures in Finland, citing per-pupil costs that can be twice as high in smaller schools, but few rigorous follow-up studies have verified whether municipalities actually realized claimed savings.

sources.title: Finnish municipal school closure cost-justification (1990s–2000s)

evidence_appendix.claim: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences regression analysis with matching methods (2010–2018 period) found that Finnish rural community population declined *more* after school closure than before, suggesting closures accelerate rather than merely follow demographic decline.

sources.title: Finnish school closure and community population decline (DID/matching study, 2010–2018)

evidence_appendix.claim: A quasi-experimental study of 600 students displaced from rural Finnish schools closed for cost reasons (not performance failure) in 1999–2000 found measurable short-term achievement disruption from consolidation itself, isolating the 'pure consolidation shock' independent of prior school quality.

sources.title: Finnish school consolidation displacement effect (cost-driven closures, 1999–2000)

evidence_appendix.claim: School choice and decentralization in Finland (1990s onward) are associated with rising school segregation in metropolitan areas, but the causal weighting between choice-driven enrollment patterns versus residential sorting remains contested among Finnish scholars.

sources.title: Finnish school choice and segregation debate (Helsinki metropolitan area)

evidence_appendix.claim: School maintenance authority for villages under 3,000 inhabitants is held by tankerületi centers (school district centers), not by municipal governments, eliminating village-level formal authority over school closure decisions.

sources.title: Hungarian school governance centralization (2013-2016 Klebelsberg Intézményfenntartó Központ and tankerület restructuring)

evidence_appendix.claim: Tankerületi centers continue to apply efficiency and enrollment ratios as criteria for closing small village schools, as documented in 2022 closure decisions in the Sopron region.

sources.title: Sopron Tankerület Center closure decisions and resident responses (Babót, Osli, Agyagosszergény)

evidence_appendix.claim: National class-size and per-pupil funding formulas create structural fiscal pressure on small village schools independent of governance structure, with minimum class sizes (14-27 students for primary grades) and funding reductions when enrollment falls below legal maximum thresholds.

sources.title: Annex 4 of Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education; June tankerület leaders discussion on funding-hour impacts

evidence_appendix.claim: As of 2024, more than 1,200 of Hungary's 3,155 settlements (38%) have no public educational institution, raising territorial equity concerns within a context of 3.4% national population decline over the prior decade.

sources.title: 2022 Hungarian Census; settlement-level educational infrastructure audit (2024)

evidence_appendix.claim: An incoming 2026 governance reform will restructure but not abolish the tankerület system, with plans to restore principal autonomy and municipal input while retaining centralized multi-tier authority over closure decisions.

sources.title: 2026 Hungarian government education reform statements and party program

evidence_appendix.claim: Kindergarten coverage gaps (31% of municipalities with no kindergarten in 2022) mirror and compound primary-school access problems in the same small villages, governed by the same national framework with enrollment thresholds (8-child minimum) rather than local discretion.

sources.title: 2022 Hungarian kindergarten infrastructure assessment

evidence_appendix.claim: School closure and merger decision authority in Hungary is vested in a centralized state actor (Klebelsberg Center) rather than municipalities or local communities, which constrains the feasibility of locally-sequenced intervention mechanisms.

sources.title: Hungarian centralization of school governance (2013 onwards)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary has experienced repeated historical cycles of rural school expansion and contraction (interwar expansion, Stalinist demolition 1948+, 1970s rationalization) without establishing a durable institutionalized feedback mechanism, suggesting past sequences were driven by top-down political decisions rather than standing demographic-trigger rules.

sources.title: Historical cycles of Hungarian rural school policy (interwar, Stalinist, 1970s, 1960s farmstead school closures)

evidence_appendix.claim: In 2016, school directors and county self-government associations explicitly flagged risks of closure or consolidation via centralized school-center formation, but no formal consultation or appeals mechanism was reported to exist at that time.

sources.title: 2016 ministry consultation gap on school consolidation

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's population has declined by nearly 8% since 1990, with projections indicating a further 13% decline by 2050, but this decline is spatially uneven, with North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western regions experiencing more acute depopulation than national average.

sources.title: Hungarian depopulation trends and regional variation (KSH demographic data, 2023 projections)

evidence_appendix.claim: International legal safeguards for segregation prevention (ECtHR 2013 judgment on special-school segregation, 2023 Piliscsaba village-school judgment) have not been effectively enforced domestically over a decade, as the Committee of Ministers monitoring has not closed the 2013 case despite repeated non-closure findings.

sources.title: ECtHR segregation judgments (2013, 2023) and Committee of Ministers monitoring

evidence_appendix.claim: The Hungarian teaching workforce is ageing (50% of teachers are age 50+, only 7.2% under 30) and is experiencing documented attrition, particularly in schools serving ethnic-minority students, with a 2023 Status Law change permitting involuntary teacher transfers to address shortages.

sources.title: Hungarian teacher demographics and labor-relations reform (2024/2025 academic year; July 2023 Status Law)

evidence_appendix.claim: While free public transport for children up to age 14 was introduced in March 2023, rural and small-settlement transport frequencies remain limited, particularly in North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western Hungary—regions that overlap with areas of highest demographic depopulation.

sources.title: Hungarian transport tariff reform (March 2023) and rural transit coverage (KSH analysis)

evidence_appendix.claim: Institutionalized stakeholder-consultation mechanisms in Hungarian school governance were suspended or curtailed after 2010 as part of broader centralization, affecting the procedural architecture available for local-level input in school-network rationalization decisions.

sources.title: Hungarian education centralization and stakeholder-consultation curtailment post-2010

evidence_appendix.claim: School closures in depopulating rural areas are followed by accelerated local depopulation rather than simply resulting from pre-existing demographic decline, based on difference-in-differences regression modelling of Finnish municipalities 2010–2018 and corroborating evidence from Saxony

sources.title: Lehtonen (2021); Sørensen et al. (2021) systematic review on depopulation and rural school closure

evidence_appendix.claim: Scotland's 'presumption against rural school closure' (Children and Young People Scotland Act 2014) requires education authorities to formally assess alternatives to closure, including steps that would avoid closure entirely, and to document likely educational benefits, community effects, and travelling arrangements

sources.title: Children and Young People (Scotland) Act 2014, section 11A; Scottish government guidance on rural school closure (2010 Act revised)

evidence_appendix.claim: France's conventions ruralité establish reciprocal three-year contracts between State (via rector and prefect) and local elected officials (mayors' associations, departmental councils), in which the State maintains teaching posts and staffing allowances while municipalities commit to reorganizing school networks, with co-financing available via rural territorial equipment grants

sources.title: French education inspection-general study on conventions ruralité 2010–2019; Senate report on rural education contracting

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's 2013 centralization via the Public Education Act transferred school management, budgeting, staffing and curriculum control from municipalities to the central Klebelsberg Institution Maintenance Centre (KLIK), eliminating the municipal bargaining power and budget autonomy that most European closure-prevention mechanisms presume

sources.title: Hungarian Public Education Act 2013; contemporary reporting on KLIK centralization and teacher employment

evidence_appendix.claim: Wales adopted a non-presumptive guidance model for rural school closures, requiring consideration of alternatives such as school federation rather than a legal presumption against closure, contrasting with Scotland's and England's presumption-based approaches

sources.title: Welsh government guidance on rural school closures and federation models (2013 comparative briefing)

evidence_appendix.claim: School closures in Sweden (2013–2020) demonstrated measurable negative political effects: closures decreased trust in municipal politicians and reduced electoral support for incumbents, with effects most pronounced for left-leaning parties, suggesting political costs may drive local resistance to closures independent of educational rationale

sources.title: Swedish geocoded dataset study of rural school closures 2013–2020 and electoral effects

evidence_appendix.claim: In Hungary, community mobilization through the NGO 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' and village-level protests (Erdőkürt case) contested school closures in the absence of formal legal protections, while in Poland, village-level protests in 2000 resulted in mixed outcomes: two of four schools were saved and two closed

sources.title: Hungarian case study (Erdőkürt); Polish multiple-case study of four villages (2000 protests)

evidence_appendix.claim: Austria's documented policy approach to unavoidable rural school closures emphasizes 'gestures of reconciliation'—managing social aftermath through building repurposing, commemorative ceremonies, and negotiated closure timing—rather than preventing closure, representing a distinct logic from closure-prevention mechanisms in Scotland or France

sources.title: Literature on 'Gestures of reconciliation: the case of small primary school closures in rural Austria'

evidence_appendix.claim: Systematic review of rural depopulation policy explicitly notes that almost all literature covers only European regions and that all reviewed policies target depopulation reduction as a quantitative variable, but no study addresses qualitative aspects of depopulation reduction, limiting scalability and validation for non-European contexts

sources.title: Systematic review on rural depopulation and school closure (scope limitation caveat)

evidence_appendix.claim: Until 2013, Hungarian primary schools were maintained by municipalities; Act CXC of 2011 centralized this duty to a state agency (Klebelsberg School Maintainer Centre) effective 1 January 2013, terminating schools as separate legal entities and merging them into KLIK.

sources.title: Hungarian Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education; structural reform documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: Government Decree 134/2016 restructured maintainer authority by creating 59 school district centers (tankerületi központok) subordinate to KLIK, with district councils having only advisory input and school directors appointed by the Minister of Education, not selected locally.

sources.title: Government Decree 134/2016; KLIK administrative restructuring (2017)

evidence_appendix.claim: As of Government Decree 182/2022, the Ministry of Interior (not an education ministry) became responsible for oversight of public education, including the Klebelsberg Center and school district system.

sources.title: Government Decree 182/2022 (V.24.); Hungarian government reshuffle 2022

evidence_appendix.claim: Small village schools are closed administratively via 'temporary closure' or 'suspension of educational activity' decisions by school district centers citing low enrollment and cost, without evidence of a dedicated statutory closure procedure specific to demographic decline.

sources.title: Sopron District Center statements (2022); Debrecen school district reporting (2024)

evidence_appendix.claim: The legal responsibility for organizing pupil transport to consolidated schools is disputed: the Interior Ministry states it is a municipal duty, but an ombudsman inquiry found the legal environment does not clearly assign this obligation to either the municipality or the school district.

sources.title: Interior Ministry Communications Department statement; Deputy Ombudsman inquiry (Tiszavasvár/Bűd case)

evidence_appendix.claim: When a church actor seeks to assume maintainer rights of a state-run school, more than half of parents must support the transfer, per the state secretary's procedural statement; uptake of this safeguard for village schools threatened by depopulation has not been evidenced.

sources.title: State secretariat for public education procedural statement; Act CLXXIX of 2011 on minority rights

evidence_appendix.claim: National minority self-governments can become school maintainers under Act CLXXIX of 2011 where 75% of students receive minority education; as of September 2012, three schools had been transferred to the National Roma Self-Government, but uptake remains limited.

sources.title: Act CLXXIX of 2011 on rights of minorities; National Roma Self-Government records (2012)

evidence_appendix.claim: Closure and merger decisions by school district centers have been reversed through political-administrative intervention after public protest, without formal legal appeal, indicating an informal accountability channel not codified in statutory procedure.

sources.title: Budapest school closure case (2025–2026); county government commissioner intervention; district municipal education committee opposition

evidence_appendix.claim: As of May 2026, the Hungarian government reportedly plans a structural overhaul (not abolition) of the Klebelsberg Center and school-district system to restore school-director autonomy and municipal/local community input, citing daily operational dysfunction (e.g., inability to hire substitute teachers or approve lightbulb replacement without district permission).

sources.title: Government spokesperson statement (May 2026); reported structural reform plans

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 1993 decentralization transferred school management to municipalities (gminas), creating fiscal exposure that made small rural schools vulnerable to local closure decisions rather than national policy direction.

sources.title: Polish Ministry of National Education decentralization and school governance transfer, 1989–1993

evidence_appendix.claim: Approximately 3,300 Polish schools closed during 2000–2016, with declining fertility and migration cited as primary drivers, combined with economic reasons as direct closure justifications at local level.

sources.title: Polish school closures 2000–2016: Demographic and economic drivers

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 1999 gimnazjum reform (creating larger lower-secondary schools with expanded catchment areas) functioned as a structural reorganization lever distinct from simple closure, consolidating pupil access across neighbouring villages while formally preserving some primary buildings.

sources.title: Polish grade-structure reform and rural school network reorganization, 1999

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2017 reversal of the gimnazjum reform (returning to eight-year primary schools) was explicitly political and populist in rationale, disregarding positive PISA outcomes and labour-market evidence supporting the 1999 reform.

sources.title: Polish education reform reversal 2017: Political rationale vs. evidence

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2017 reform reversal implementation caused acute disruption: closure of ~7,000 junior high schools, potential displacement of 10,000 teachers, and a double-cohort bottleneck in high-school admissions (e.g., 3,173 students unplaced in Warsaw alone).

sources.title: Polish education reform reversal 2017: Implementation disruption and transition costs

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2025 draft legislation on small schools proposes repurposing infrastructure (e.g., crèches, community services) rather than binary closure, framing rural schools as social/cultural centres and adapting to demographic change while protecting proximity to family home.

sources.title: Polish small schools draft legislation 2025

evidence_appendix.claim: Cross-national causal evidence on school closure and population decline is contested: Polish village-level studies found smaller structural social-capital decline where schools were saved, while Finnish and Swedish studies found school closure correlated with or unrelated to population decline respectively.

sources.title: Causal effects of school closure on rural depopulation: Comparative evidence

evidence_appendix.claim: Since 2013, municipalities lost formal authority over school maintenance to a central agency (KLIK), shifting closure/keep-open decisions from locally accountable mayors to a national bureaucracy nominally insulated from village-level electoral pressure.

sources.title: Act CXC on National Public Education (2011) and KLIK establishment (2013)

evidence_appendix.claim: Church takeover of village schools is an established alternative-operator coalition for keeping schools nominally open; expansion of denominational sector grew 60% (2001-2011) and a further 47% (2011-2014) in small towns and villages.

sources.title: TÁRKI Societal Report; education ministry amendment records

evidence_appendix.claim: A documented case study shows local coalitions can save a school, but success is contingent on rare social capital (bonding and bridging networks within villages).

sources.title: Peer-reviewed Hungarian village case study on school-rescue operations

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's Magyar Falu Program (Village Program) has provided 600 localities with 10 billion forints (EUR 28.3 million) for communal development, and PM Orbán has personally framed rural revival as a governing narrative.

sources.title: Hungarian Village Program funding records; PM Orbán statements

evidence_appendix.claim: KLIK centralization was justified on cost-equalization grounds but has produced documented budget failures: KLIK has been in deficit since its first year despite receiving billions of forints annually, and previously better-off urban schools experienced substantial funding declines.

sources.title: KLIK solvency records; education funding analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2025 Budapest case shows that central district-level school-merger decisions can be reversed after local mobilization (4,000-signature petition and two demonstrations), when a district centre withdrew its reorganization proposal.

sources.title: Budapest district school authority (Külső-Pesti Tankerületi Központ) reorganization reversal

evidence_appendix.claim: A nationwide teacher mobilization in 2022-2023 created a union/parent/student coalition (October 2022 demonstrations drew 50,000+ participants) opposing government education policy, raising the political cost of any school reorganization.

sources.title: Hungarian teacher union (PDSZ) campaigns; 2022 education protests

evidence_appendix.claim: Long-run Hungarian evidence shows rural school-network size has swung repeatedly across political regimes: communist-era centralization (1950s-1970s) reduced primary schools from 6,000+ to 4,200 in 10 years; church schools were reduced from 4,100+ to ten after nationalization.

sources.title: Hungarian historical education records; Communist-era education policy

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary centralized school governance and maintenance in 2013 under KLIK, then restructured again in 2017 to 58-60 school district centres, removing local municipal control over small-village school viability decisions and separating villages from ordinary municipal funding logic.

sources.title: Hungarian Public Education Act 2013 and school maintenance restructuring

evidence_appendix.claim: Disadvantaged children are increasingly concentrated in rural settlements, with up to 94% disadvantage rates in certain rural settlements, driven partly by spatial selectivity, catchment policies, and school closures in peripheral areas.

sources.title: Settlement-level clustering study 2021-2024 on Hungarian depopulation types

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian primary teachers earn 34% less than tertiary-educated workers (OECD comparison: 17% less), and a 32% average salary increase was implemented in early 2024, but Hungary continues to face severe teacher shortage and aging workforce.

sources.title: OECD teacher salary comparisons and Hungarian education workforce data

evidence_appendix.claim: Multigrade and mixed-age teaching models in Chile (Puentes Educativos) and Norway show quality potential when supported by deliberate teacher training, cross-school networks, and professional learning communities, but these are non-Hungarian systems.

sources.title: Comparative pedagogy: multigrade teaching in Chile and Norway

evidence_appendix.claim: Church-run schools in Hungary have doubled since 2010, enjoy student-selection freedom and greater autonomy, whereas state-run schools must accept all catchment students; this potentially concentrates disadvantage in remaining state village schools.

sources.title: Hungarian church-school expansion and enrollment trends

evidence_appendix.claim: European Court of Human Rights ruled in Szolcsán v. Hungary (2023) that Roma segregation in schools breaches discrimination prohibition; EU infringement proceedings ongoing since 2016; catchment-based school network decisions are subject to non-discrimination legal constraints.

sources.title: Szolcsán v. Hungary (2023) and EU infringement proceedings

evidence_appendix.claim: A single documented case (Erdőkürt village school) shows uneven local social capital and bonding/bridging networks determined school survival, suggesting community-reliant non-structural strategies would produce uneven outcomes rather than uniform solutions across villages.

sources.title: Erdőkürt case study on social capital and rural school retention

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's population declined by 334,000 individuals (3.4%) over the decade preceding the 2022 census, the largest inter-census drop on record, directly shrinking the school-age cohort feeding rural primary schools nationwide.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) 2022 Census

evidence_appendix.claim: Nearly 18% of Hungary's population lived in the capital, almost 53% in towns, and 30% in villages as of 1 January 2024; migration gains in villages declined significantly from 2023 onward, reversing a pandemic-era counter-urbanization trend.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) 2024 Population Distribution and Migration Data

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's primary school network contracted from above 6,000 schools to nearly 4,200 in a 10-year period (1960s–70s) under Communist-era rationalization policy, establishing historical precedent for top-down network consolidation independent of demographic demand alone.

sources.title: Hungarian Education History: School Rationalization (1960s–70s)

evidence_appendix.claim: Since 2013, school maintenance has been centralized under the Public Education Act; the Klebelsberg Institution Maintenance Centre (KLIK) controls finances, teacher employment, and curriculum across 198 school districts, removing municipal authority over closure and merger decisions.

sources.title: Hungarian Public Education Act 2013 and KLIK Administrative Structure

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2007 wave of rural school closures and mergers in Hungary, including cases in Nógrád county (Mátraszőlős, Erdőkürt), showed closure cascades where neighboring villages with enrollments of 35–65 pupils operated mixed-year classes, with nearby surviving schools facing 'association pressure' (társulás) as child numbers fell.

sources.title: 2007 Hungarian Rural School Closure Wave; Human Chain for Rural Schools NGO Campaign

evidence_appendix.claim: Comparative international research on school closure and population decline is contested: a Finnish DID/matching study (2010–2018) found population decreased more after closure, but Barakat (2015, 2014) in Saxony and Amcoff (2012) in Sweden found no significant causal link; a 2022 study found only 17% of residents in municipalities without an elementary school considered its absence a major shortcoming.

sources.title: Comparative European School Closure and Population Studies (Finland, Saxony, Sweden); Bernard, Joukl, Vítková 2022

evidence_appendix.claim: Rural areas in Hungary's North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western regions have uneven public transport access; frequency and scheduling in small settlements make public transport impractical for daily school commutes, with a significant gap between Budapest and rural areas in barrier-free transport infrastructure development.

sources.title: Hungarian Rural Transport Infrastructure Assessment

evidence_appendix.claim: A minority-language school bus program transports more than 500 Hungarian-minority students across 13 Transylvanian counties using vehicles seating 8–21 people, demonstrating a multi-village, route-based transport logistics model; direct applicability to domestic Hungarian rural consolidation is inferred but not confirmed.

sources.title: Transylvanian Hungarian-Minority School Transport Scheme 2024–2025

evidence_appendix.claim: In a Hungarian micro-region, settlements with disproportionately high Roma population shares exhibited corresponding school-level Roma enrollment disparities; neighboring settlements perceived successful schools' pupil 'pull' as a threat to their own enrollment, creating informal inter-municipal competition that distorts simple geographic-efficiency models.

sources.title: Ethnic Segregation and School Network Dynamics in Hungarian Micro-Regions

evidence_appendix.claim: Across multiple European countries including Finland, municipalities have targeted rural schools with low enrollment for closure citing cost-cutting and out-migration, with Hungarian literature noting that closures in marginalized areas often intensified social isolation and ethnic segregation; however, the causal link between closure and further population decline remains contested.

sources.title: European Municipal School Closure Policy and Rural Community Disintegration (Finland comparative study)

content.generation_notice: Fresh live v2 production replicate using the admitted lens registry. Generator=anthropic; judge=openai; human external-use gate remains pending.
content.public_question: What should happen to primary schools in depopulating villages?
content.summary:
Hungary's village primary schools sit inside a governance structure where maintenance authority rests with centralized tankerületi centers rather than municipalities [F-live-hungarian_education_system-01], closures proceed via undocumented administrative acts rather than a dedicated statutory procedure [F-live-legal_and_governance-04], and 38% of settlements already lack any public educational institution [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06]. Six transformation options define the option space, distinguished by where they intervene and what they leave unresolved.

T1 (presumption-against-closure) and T2 (reciprocal state-municipality contracts) both add procedural friction before closure without transferring decision authority. T1 replicates Scotland's mandatory-alternatives-assessment model [F-live-international_comparison-02], but Scotland's mechanism presumes a local actor with standing to invoke it—standing Hungary's 2013 centralization eliminated [F-live-international_comparison-04]. T2 replicates France's conventions ruralité [F-live-international_comparison-03], but French communes retain fiscal and legal standing Hungarian municipalities lack after centralization [F-live-legal_and_governance-01][F-live-legal_and_governance-02]. Both risk becoming formalities unless paired with genuine veto power or an independent oversight body, since existing closure reversals occur only through informal political intervention, not codified appeal [F-live-legal_and_governance-08][F-live-political_feasibility-07].

T3 (grade-split: village lower grades, centralized upper grades) and T4 (networked clusters/multi-service hubs) operate at the structural/pedagogical rather than authority level. T3 follows Poland's 1999 gimnazjum logic [F-live-polish_reform-03], targeting the empirically supported finding that younger children suffer most from long commutes [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10], but Poland's 2017 reversal—politically driven despite positive PISA and labour-market evidence—caused severe disruption (7,000 schools closed, 10,000 teachers displaced) [F-live-polish_reform-04][F-live-polish_reform-05], flagging high reversal risk. T4 draws on French clustering [F-live-international_comparison-03] and Poland's 2025 draft repurposing legislation [F-live-polish_reform-06], but its only Hungarian precedent (Erdőkürt) succeeded through rare, unevenly distributed local social capital [F-live-portuguese_reform-10], an empirical uncertainty about whether formal structures can substitute for it.

T5 (default closure with mitigation) and T6 (evidence/transparency infrastructure) accept different baselines. T5 treats closure as default where enrollment thresholds are unmet, compensating via transport and equity support—directly relevant given transport-responsibility is currently legally unresolved between the Interior Ministry and municipalities [F-live-legal_and_governance-05], and given Roma children's 45% share of segregated-school enrollment [F-live-education_finance-06]. But whether closure itself accelerates depopulation is a genuine empirical uncertainty: Danish evidence finds a causal 7.6-point population decline over ten years [F-live-demography-07], Finnish quasi-experimental evidence corroborates acceleration [F-live-finnish_reform-05][F-live-finnish_reform-06], while Saxon and Swedish studies find no link [F-live-education_finance-04]. T6 proposes a national viability registry and cost-visibility rules to resolve exactly this kind of contested evidence, including unverified consolidation-savings claims [F-live-education_finance-02][F-live-finnish_reform-04] and routinely undercounted transport cost offsets [F-live-education_finance-03]. It is the most institutionally compatible with the status quo since it requires no authority transfer, but Hungary's own history of top-down political decisions overriding technical evidence—as in Poland's 2017 reversal [F-live-polish_reform-04]—is a value/political question about whether information alone changes behavior, not merely an empirical one.

Material disciplinary mechanisms threading through all options include: the national class-size and per-pupil funding formula that creates fiscal pressure independent of governance form [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04]; the zero-enrollment cliff observed in the smallest settlements [F-live-demography-05] amid a 22% birth-cohort collapse since 2021 [F-live-demography-01]; the 2026 tankerület restructuring as a legislative vehicle that preserves rather than abolishes centralized authority [F-live-hungarian_education_system-07]; and documented inter-municipal competition over pupil enrollment that could undermine voluntary cooperation models [F-live-school_network_planning-09].

Decision-makers must choose between: procedural safeguard versus genuine authority transfer; protecting the youngest children locally versus resolving structural contraction; individual-level mitigation versus community-level preservation; and building evidence infrastructure now versus acting under demographic time pressure. The research most likely to change this choice concerns whether closure causally drives depopulation in the Hungarian context specifically, whether the 2026 reform alters funding penalties or grants any local standing in closure decisions, and whether a codified presumption would actually bind the same centralized authority currently exercising discretion.
content.title: The Future of Rural Primary Schools
